edge: Data scraping is a method used to obtain datasets from social media platforms.
Poisoning attacks are proposed as a defense against unauthorized data scraping
These attacks aim to prevent unauthorized individuals from using scraped data.
Existing data poisoning methods require knowledge of the target architecture to create a surrogate network.
Surrogate networks are trained using the complete dataset to generate the attack.
The proposed AR poisoning method bypasses the need for dataset access and is more versatile.
AR perturbations can be applied universally across different datasets without knowledge of the entire set.
AR poisons are resistant to common defenses like adversarial training.
They also withstand strong data augmentations employed to protect models.
Personal data collected from media sources is used to train facial recognition systems.

Performance is stated to vary across different architectures.

CutMix is another strong augmentation mentioned alongside Cutout.
Mixup is also listed as a strong augmentation.
AR poisons are said to degrade test performance more than other types of poisons.
AR perturbations can mitigate some effects of adversarial training.
The paper discusses the importance of quickly learned poisons and their harmful effect.

The context describes a sliding window being used in conjunction with an autoregressive (AR) process. The AR process initializes the sliding window with Gaussian noise and computes subsequent entries using previous values.
Initial observations are required for the AR process
Gaussian noise is used to initialize the first V entries in the sliding window.
An AR(V-1) process is applied within the sliding window
The sliding window procedure is similar to a convolution

Autoregressive signals are proposed to be easy to learn because they can be classified perfectly by a simple CNN
A simple CNN is designed to classify AR perturbations perfectly by using AR filters
The coefficients of an AR(p) model determine the characteristics of the process
An AR filter is designed to have a zero response when applied to an AR perturbation
The focus is on creating imperceptible perturbations that are easy for CNNs to learn
The AR filter is designed to correctly identify the AR process from which the perturbation was generated.
A 3-layer CNN can perfectly classify AR perturbations

The context discusses the use of different perturbation radii (⇢a) in adversarial training
Data poisoning methods are shown to be fragile against adversarial training when the training radius (⇢a) is smaller than the poisoning radius.
The trade-off between standard test accuracy and robust test accuracy becomes more exaggerated as the adversarial training radius increases.
Poisons become less effective as the adversarial training radius grows; at ⇢a = 0.5
AR perturbations remain more effective at smaller radii (⇢a = 0.125 and ⇢a = 0.25) compared to other poisons.
In a practical scenario
The effectiveness of a poison is assessed by its impact on test accuracy when mixed with varying proportions of clean data.
A poison is considered effective if its inclusion in training decreases test accuracy compared to training solely on clean data.
The context discusses the evaluation of the effectiveness of poisons with different proportions of clean and poisoned data. AR poisons remain effective even when mixed with clean data.

AR poisoning is different from these techniques as it doesn't require a broader dataset or surrogate network parameters.
These perturbations are designed to be so simple that they can be perfectly classified by a manually-specified 3-layer CNN.
The goal of AR poisoning is to prevent a network from generalizing to the clean test distribution.
A practitioner is advised to avoid using AR poisoned data if they have access to the clean data.
